scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule is attributed to the laws of Numa, the second king of Rome, within a work documenting the Early Roman Kingdom.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Plutarch describes the establishment and authority of the Fetial priests under Numa Pompilius.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Numa is credited with establishing the Fetial college early in his reign (traditional span -715 to -673).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This religious prohibition on labor during processions is part of the Numaic reforms in early Rome.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The rule is part of the religious calendar and ritual order established by Numa during his reign (midpoint of Numa's reign is approx -694; -713 used as the founding era).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Describes land redistribution following Romulus's conquests during Numa's reign.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This redistribution occurred at the start of Numa's reign (-715) to transition the bellicose Romulan society to agriculture.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Describes the administrative and social mobility practices under Numa Pompilius.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Plutarch notes this as an amendment to earlier Roman law (the patria potestas) made by Numa.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the reign of Numa Pompilius (-715 to -673), specifically as a reform of the preceding Romulan laws.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Describes the ritual bathing of women in Rome, attributed by Plutarch to the Numaic calendar.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Numa is traditionally credited with the reform of the Roman calendar and the institution of these monthly rituals at the start of his reign.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Relates to the oral transmission of Numa's sacred books to the Roman priests.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim explicitly identifies this as a rule of the 'Pythagoreans', a distinct philosophical community.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Though mentioned in Numa, the rule refers to the Pythagorean school, traditionally founded around -530.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Relates to the composition of the Senate during the reign of Numa Pompilius.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This occurred when Marcius accompanied Numa to Rome at the start of his reign.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim names Petilius as 'praetor', an office of the Republic, acting against rediscovered Numaic books.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The discovery and burning of Numa's books by the praetor Petilius is a historical event dated to -181.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Describes a legal restriction on the dictator Fabius Maximus during the Second Punic War.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Fabius Maximus was appointed dictator in -217 following the Battle of Lake Trasimene.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Refers to the constitutional precedence of the Dictator over the Consul during the Republic.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This specific interaction occurred when Fabius Maximus asserted his authority over the consul Gnaeus Servilius Geminus in -217.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Describes the standard ceremonial escort for high Roman magistrates (Dictators and Consuls).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Fabius Maximus's dictatorship in -217, when he reclaimed the ancient right of twenty-four lictors.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Describes the legal immunity of the Tribune of the Plebs, Metilius, during the dictatorship of Fabius Maximus.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Refers to the absolute legal powers of the Roman Dictator during the Republic.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Describes a prisoner exchange convention during the Second Punic War.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Describes the rotation of command between Consuls Terentius Varro and Lucius Aemilius Paullus.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Describes the role of a young patrician (Lentulus) serving in the cavalry, a common class-based military role.
